speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're talking about publishing a static website with GitHub Pages, which is one of the simplest ways to take files from a repository and make them available on the web.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is especially useful for documentation, workshop materials, portfolios, project home pages, and other sites that don't need a back-end server, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub Pages is built into GitHub. For a simple site, you don't need to rent a server or build a complicated deployment pipeline. You can publish directly from a repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we go deeper, I want to make sure we frame it for our learners. This appendix is a reference companion to earlier material on open source culture and also connects to later next-step work. And the main authoritative source behind the material is GitHub's Pages documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. So think of this lesson as practical, but also grounded in the official docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here's the basic idea. GitHub Pages hosts static files. That means files are served as they already exist: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, images, PDFs, and similar assets.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when people hear website, they might imagine a full web app. But GitHub Pages is not running server-side code for you.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the key distinction. No PHP, no Python app server, no Node request handlers, no database-backed logic running on the server. If your project needs dynamic server behavior, GitHub Pages is not the right host by itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But for static content, it's great.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very often, yes. It's good for documentation sites, workshop materials like this project, project landing pages, personal portfolios, and simple blogs, including some Jekyll-based workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the URL pattern matters too. Usually the published address looks like username dot github dot io, slash repository name, slash.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In the common case, it's: username dot github dot io slash repository-name slash. For a user site or an organization profile repository named username slash username dot github dot io, the published site becomes just username dot github dot io.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk setup. If I'm on GitHub dot com and I want to enable Pages for a repository, what do I do?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to the repository. Open Settings. In the left sidebar, find Pages under the Code and automation group. Then under Build and deployment, choose how the site will be published.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are two major choices.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. One is Deploy from a branch. The other is GitHub Actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I choose deploy from a branch, I then choose a branch like main or master, and a folder source.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The folder choices are the repository root, shown as slash, or the docs folder, shown as slash docs. Then you select the combination and choose Save.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after that, GitHub usually builds and deploys within a minute or two, and once the first deployment succeeds, the site URL appears near the top of the Pages settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the typical flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the accessibility walk-through too, because that matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. For screen reader users, the Settings control is a link in the repository's top navigation and has the accessible name Settings. Inside repository settings, the Pages link is in the left sidebar under the Code and automation grouping. The branch and folder controls are standard select elements, so you can navigate them with arrow keys.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low-vision users, the Pages screen follows the same general settings layout GitHub uses elsewhere. You click Settings, then look in the left sidebar for Pages. The deployment status uses familiar visual signals like a green check for success and a red X for failure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Also important: the published website does not inherit accessibility styling from GitHub itself. Your site's own CSS has to provide readable font sizing, adequate contrast, visible focus indicators, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted mouse users, it's still just Settings, then Pages, then choose the branch and folder source, then Save. After success, there's generally a green banner with the site URL.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's compare publishing sources a little more carefully. Deploying from a branch is the simplest model. GitHub reads files directly from the branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the two folder modes behave differently.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. If you choose root, GitHub serves the repository root. If you choose docs, it serves only the docs folder. Using docs is often the better practice because it isolates published content from source code and other repository files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Especially when a project has a build pipeline and the built site lives in a specific output folder.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when do we reach for GitHub Actions instead?

speaker: ALEX
text: When the site needs a build step before publishing. For example, if your source is Markdown and you generate HTML, or if you use a static site generator such as Jekyll, Hugo, or Eleventy, or if you want automated tests to run before deployment, including accessibility checks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in this project, there is a build step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A representative workflow here would check out the repository, run the Node script that builds the HTML, then upload the html folder as the Pages artifact.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That brings us to the project-specific part: the html folder.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. This project already has a prebuilt HTML mirror of the Markdown content in an html folder, created by scripts slash build-html dot js.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But GitHub Pages, when you use branch deployment, only supports the root folder or docs as the source folder.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the constraint. So if your output is in html, you need to adapt.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with the manual approach.

speaker: ALEX
text: Option A is manual publishing from the html folder. Since html is not an allowed branch-based Pages source folder, one safe approach is to copy the contents of html into docs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So conceptually, generate the site into html, copy those built files into docs, commit docs, push, then configure Pages to use the branch and the docs folder.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The example commands are essentially: copy recursively from html into docs, add docs to Git, commit with a message noting that HTML output was published to docs for GitHub Pages, and push.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then in Pages settings, set the source to the branch, here given as master, and the folder to slash docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a rename option, but with an important warning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Option A2 is renaming html to docs after first changing the build script output path. That could work in a project where docs is free to use for generated output.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But not in this project.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. This project already uses docs for Markdown source files, so renaming html to docs would create a conflict. That's why copying into docs, or better yet using GitHub Actions, is safer here.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack the GitHub Actions approach, because a lot of learners hear workflow YAML and tense up.

speaker: ALEX
text: Fair. The purpose of the workflow is straightforward: on each push to the chosen branch, GitHub runs a series of steps to rebuild the site and deploy it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: In the example workflow, the trigger is a push to master.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The workflow requests permissions needed for Pages deployment, including reading contents, writing Pages data, and using an ID token. It also sets a concurrency group for pages so deployments are managed predictably.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there are two jobs: build and deploy.

speaker: ALEX
text: The build job runs on Ubuntu, checks out the repository, sets up Node version 20, installs dependencies with npm install, runs node scripts slash build-html dot js, and uploads the html folder as the Pages artifact.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the deploy job waits for build, then deploys that artifact using the official deploy-pages action.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That means you don't have to copy files manually into docs. The workflow turns html into the deployed site automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is cleaner and less error-prone once you're comfortable with Actions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually, yes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now custom domains. A lot of people don't want the default github dot io address forever.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Pages supports custom domains, so instead of something like community-access dot github dot io slash Learning-Room, you can use a domain you control, like a subdomain or apex domain.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the setup sequence?

speaker: ALEX
text: In repository Settings, then Pages, enter the domain in the Custom domain field and save. GitHub creates a CNAME file in the repository root containing that domain name. Then you configure DNS with your domain provider.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the DNS record type depends on whether it's a subdomain or an apex domain.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. For a subdomain like learning dot community-access dot org, you typically create a CNAME pointing to the GitHub Pages host, here illustrated as community-access dot github dot io. For an apex domain like community-access dot org, you use A records.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the current A record IPs listed here are four addresses: 185 point 199 point 108 point 153, then 109 point 153, 110 point 153, and 111 point 153.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. And DNS changes may take up to 48 hours to propagate, so this is one of those places where patience matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then GitHub checks and verifies the domain once DNS is configured.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and there's another security step learners should know: domain verification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's to reduce domain takeover risk?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub recommends verifying your custom domain in your account or organization settings under Pages. That way, if the domain is ever temporarily removed from one repo, another party has a harder time claiming it for their own GitHub Pages site.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's move into HTTPS and security more broadly.

speaker: ALEX
text: For github dot io subdomains, HTTPS is enforced automatically. For custom domains, once DNS has propagated, you should enable Enforce HTTPS in Pages settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Pages uses Let's Encrypt to provision the certificate automatically.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. When HTTPS is enforced, HTTP traffic is redirected to HTTPS.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a big security reminder here: do not put secrets into a GitHub Pages repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. No API keys, no private credentials, no confidential data. A GitHub Pages site serves public content. Treat everything in the published site and its repository as potentially public unless the hosting model and repo settings clearly guarantee otherwise.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now to one of the most important parts for this audience: accessibility of the published site itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Publishing accessible source material is not enough if the resulting website becomes harder to navigate after deployment. The published site should meet the same accessibility expectations as the source.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So let's go point by point.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigation and landmarks. Use meaningful landmark regions and semantic structure so screen reader users can move efficiently among major areas of the page. That usually means a clear header, navigation region, main content, and footer where appropriate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And headings should reflect the content hierarchy, not just visual styling.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next: skip navigation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Provide a skip link so keyboard and screen reader users can move past repeated navigation and get to the main content quickly. It should be keyboard reachable and visible on focus.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then focus management, especially if the site behaves like a single-page app or has in-page navigation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If navigation changes the visible content without a full page reload, manage focus intentionally so users understand that context has changed. Don't leave keyboard focus stranded in an old control while the page appears to have moved somewhere else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Images are another big one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Every meaningful image needs appropriate alt text. Decorative images should not create noise for assistive technology. The rule is not just add alt text everywhere. It's use the right text alternative for the function and meaning of the image.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Color contrast comes up a lot with static sites because people may assume a simple site is automatically accessible.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is not automatic. Your CSS needs sufficient color contrast, readable text sizing, and visible interactive states. GitHub Pages does not fix poor contrast for you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And testing has to happen after deployment, not just before.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Test the published site itself. Sometimes the generated HTML, asset paths, deployment context, or production CSS changes what users experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are some good post-deployment checks?

speaker: ALEX
text: At minimum: verify page titles are correct and unique enough to orient users, confirm landmarks and heading structure still make sense, tab through navigation and interactive controls, test the skip link, check focus visibility, inspect image alt behavior, confirm color contrast in the real published theme, and try the site with a screen reader on the actual deployed URL.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the accessibility learning cards here really boil down to this: the hosted site is its own user experience, and it has to be evaluated as such.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does this connect back to GitHub Actions and continuous deployment?

speaker: ALEX
text: Continuous deployment means every push can rebuild and republish the site automatically. That's efficient, but it also means mistakes can go live quickly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is why accessibility checks in the workflow are valuable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can add automated accessibility checks to the workflow so common issues are caught in CI before or during deployment. Automation won't replace human testing, but it can flag missing alt text, obvious structural problems, and some color contrast or labeling issues depending on the tooling.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a mature workflow might build the site, run accessibility checks, and only then deploy if everything passes.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the idea.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What limitations should learners keep in mind with GitHub Pages?

speaker: ALEX
text: The biggest one is the static-only model. No server-side processing, no custom back-end logic. Also, if your source folder arrangement doesn't match Pages' branch deployment options, you may need a docs copy step or an Actions-based workflow. And custom domains and HTTPS can involve DNS timing and certificate delays that aren't under your direct control.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's wonderfully convenient, but it's not universal hosting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's finish with troubleshooting, because that's usually where stress spikes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea.

speaker: JAMIE
text: First: the site is not updating after a push.

speaker: ALEX
text: Check whether you pushed to the correct branch, whether Pages is configured for the branch and folder you intended, and whether the deployment actually succeeded. In a branch-based setup, make sure the changed files are in the published folder, not just somewhere else in the repo. In an Actions-based setup, inspect the workflow run for build or deploy failures.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Also remember that deployments may take a minute or two, and browsers can cache aggressively.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Give it a little time, then refresh carefully or test in a private window.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next: 404 on all pages except index.

speaker: ALEX
text: That often points to path issues. Internal links, asset paths, or route assumptions may work locally but break when the site is hosted under a repository subpath. Remember that many project Pages sites live under slash repository-name slash, not the domain root.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So absolute paths can be dangerous if they assume the site lives at slash.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about HTTPS certificate not provisioning?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually check the custom domain DNS first. If DNS isn't correct or hasn't fully propagated, certificate provisioning can stall. Wait for propagation, confirm records, then try enforcing HTTPS again. This is especially common right after initial domain setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the accessibility-specific troubleshooting item: a screen reader announces the wrong page title.

speaker: ALEX
text: That usually means the built HTML has incorrect title elements, duplicated templates, or client-side navigation isn't updating document titles when the visible content changes. The fix is to inspect the deployed page source and the actual browser title behavior, not just the local authoring files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So again, test the deployed experience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, I want to mention the source trail because that matters for technical learners.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The key authoritative references here are GitHub Docs broadly, the GitHub Changelog, the GitHub Pages docs, the Pages quickstart, and where relevant related Git and security documentation. For accessibility expectations, WCAG overview material is part of the factual grounding.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that section-level source map is useful as a verification tool. It tells you where to confirm each major section if GitHub changes interfaces or workflow details over time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's a strong habit for open source learners: learn the concept, then know where the living documentation lives.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the big picture is: GitHub Pages gives you a low-friction path from repository to public static site. You can publish from a branch or through Actions, adapt build outputs like html when needed, add a custom domain, enable HTTPS, and keep accessibility central from source through deployment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. And if you remember just one thing, let it be this: publishing is not the end of accessibility work. The deployed site is the product users actually experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's it for today.

speaker: ALEX
text: See you next time.
